coredata.dc:title: A reliable and facile fluorescent sensor from carbon dots for sensing 2,4,6-trinitrophenol based on inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xin
Liu, Yongli
Zhou, Qingxiang
Sheng, Xueying
Sun, Yi
Zhou, Boyao
Zhao, Jingyi
Guo, Jinghan
coredata.dc:description: 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (TNP) has absorbed much concerns because of its toxic effect and threat on the environment, which results from the fact that it is an important and universal reagent widely utilized for manufacturing many products. It is of great necessity to explore facile and efficient methods for monitoring TNP. In present study, carbon dots (CDs), a new carbonaceous nanomaterial with strong fluorescence, was applied to build a novel sensor for highly sensitive and selective detection of TNP. In the sensing procedure, the fluorescence intensity of as-prepared CDs was diminished with the presence of TNP due to inner filter effect (IFE) quenching mechanism. The sensitivity of the fluorescent sensor was very high with limit of detection down to 5.37 ng mL−1. This fluorescent sensor was evaluated and excellent spiked recoveries were gained, which demonstrated that the developed sensor would be a robust tool for environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Inner filter effect quenching mechanism
Trinitrophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Precisions and stability for sensing TNP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis of the fluorescent CDs and the working principle of the sensor.
The TEM image (A) and FTIR spectrum (B) of the obtained CDs.

Properties of the prepared CDs. (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum; (B) the contour plot (3D plot); (C) FL emission spectra; (D) effect of the pH values (1–14); (E) effect of ionic strength; (F) stability.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs (CDs, 1.0 μg mL−1; TNP, 1.0 ppm). (B) The excitation spectrum (a) and emission spectrum (b) of CDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum (c) of TNP. (C) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs with TNP (CDs, 1.0 μg mL−1; TNP, 10 μg mL−1). (D) The effect of CDs concentration. (E) The effect of buffer pH. (F) The effect of settling time.

(A) The fluorescence emission of the fluorescent sensor with different levels of TNP. (B) Plot of the values of F0/F-1 against the concentration of TNP within the range of 0.025–70 μg mL−1. (C) Regression between the values of F0/F-1 and the concentration of TNP over the range of 15–65 μg mL−1. (D) Regression between the values of F0/F-1 and the concentration of TNP over the range of 0.025–15 μg mL−1.
Selectivity of the fluorescent sensor. Quenching effect of TNP and other nitroaromatic compound (blue bars), and the mixture of TNP with each other nitro-aromatic compound (red bars), respectively.
The stability of the constructed fluorescent sensor for sensing TNP.
A comparison between the reported fluorescent sensors based on CDs and constructed sensor in present study for TNP.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ET, electron transfer; EI, electrostatic interaction; IFE, inner filter effect; FRET, fluorescence resonance energy transfer.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spiked recoveries for TNP detected by the constructed fluorescent sensor.

head.ce:title: A reliable and facile fluorescent sensor from carbon dots for sensing 2,4,6-trinitrophenol based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, School of Environment, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributes equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (TNP) has absorbed much concerns because of its toxic effect and threat on the environment, which results from the fact that it is an important and universal reagent widely utilized for manufacturing many products. It is of great necessity to explore facile and efficient methods for monitoring TNP. In present study, carbon dots (CDs), a new carbonaceous nanomaterial with strong fluorescence, was applied to build a novel sensor for highly sensitive and selective detection of TNP. In the sensing procedure, the fluorescence intensity of as-prepared CDs was diminished with the presence of TNP due to inner filter effect (IFE) quenching mechanism. The sensitivity of the fluorescent sensor was very high with limit of detection down to 5.37 ng mL−1. This fluorescent sensor was evaluated and excellent spiked recoveries were gained, which demonstrated that the developed sensor would be a robust tool for environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:para: 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (TNP), is a universal precursor in producing fireworks, explosives, dye and pharmaceuticals and so on, but it seriously threatens homeland security and public safety(Ma et al., 2012; Venkatramaiah et al., 2012; Joarder et al., 2015). For the sake of monitoring its intrinsic risks to human and environment, a variety of determination methods are being constructed to detect TNP including surface plasmon resonance-based immunosensor (Shankaran et al., 2004), infrared and Raman spectroscopy (López-López and García-Ruiz, 2014), proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (Agarwal et al., 2014), and combined methods with liquid-liquid microextraction (Burdel et al., 2013). Among them, strategies based on fluorescence exhibited their superiority due to their significant advantages of good selectivity, easy operation and low cost. Hence, fluorescence based methods are much attractive and many sensors have been established using quantum dots, new nanomaterials, dyes, metal complexes, polymer and other fluorescent reagents for TNP (Joarder et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2011; Ye et al., 2015; Sang et al., 2015; Sivaraman et al., 2014; Xiao et al., 2013; Deng et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2019; Qiao et al., 2019). However, it requires a series of complicated synthesis processes to gain the aforementioned fluorescent sensors or probes. Consequently, it is urgent to develop credible, cheap and effective sensors for TNP detection via simple and efficient approaches.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have been paid more immense concerns due to its outstanding fluorescence properties, excellent photostability, cost-effective preparation, and wonderful biocompatibility (Hou et al., 2017; Borse et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017a). The distinct benefits of CDs open the door for various uses involving detecting (Liu et al., 2016a; Wang et al., 2017a; Zhu et al., 2017a), ink (Liu et al., 2017b), bioimaging (Hamd-Ghadareh et al., 2017), drug delivery (Gao et al., 2017), photocatalysis (Zhu et al., 2017b) and optoelectronic equipment (Lin et al., 2017), etc. The obtained CDs can be used for detecting analytes upon different fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanisms that are associated with the interactions between the CDs and analytes (Zu et al., 2017). In most cases, the typical quenching mechanisms are IFE and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Ganiga et al. designed a sensitive ammonia determination method using CDs and sodium rhodizonate based on FRET quenching mechanism (Ganiga and Cyriac, 2016). In the detection system, the introduction of ammonia triggered the FRET process from CDs to sodium rhodizonate which led to decrease of the fluorescence intensity of CDs. The quenching intensity was proportional to the concentration of ammonia, which was utilized for sensing ammonia. Chen et al. prepared a kind of modified CDs using citric acid monohydrate and ethylenediamine as the precursors based on an improved microwave-assisted method, and further functionalized with 1,4,8,11-tetraazacyclotetradecane (cyclam) to form CCDs and then realized sensitive monitoring Cu2+ and S2− (Chen et al., 2016a). In the analytical procedure, the FRET can efficiently occur over them once Cu2+ was present in the system and CDs acted as the donor, and cyclam acted as the effective acceptor. The fluorescence intensity of CCDs was effectively diminished by Cu2+ attributed to FRET. Considering the strong affinity of Cu2+ to S2−, S2− made Cu2+ released from CCDs-Cu2+ complex, and then the quenched fluorescence intensity of CCDs was restored, which would be further explored to build a wonderful turn-on fluorescent sensor for sensing S2−. Liu and co-workers prepared a novel kind of CDs with sodium citrate and ammonium bicarbonate as the reactants by one pot hydrothermal method (Liu et al., 2016b). The obtained CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence quenching effect with the presence of MnO2 nanosheets through IFE, which was used to construct a good CDs-MnO2 sensor for determination of ascorbic acid. In this sensing system, ascorbic acid (AA) was added to CDs solution quenched by MnO2 nanosheets, and MnO2 nanosheets were broken owing to the strong oxidation activity of MnO2 nanosheets, and then the quenched fluorescence intensity was effectively restored. Additionally, this sensor had been applied to determination of AA in vegetables, and fresh fruits and juices. Zou et al. manufactured nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) with L-cysteine as the precursor through hydrothermal treatment (Zou et al., 2017). The fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs was significantly reduced by fluazinam because of the strong IFE. This phenomenon was applied to develop a sensitive sensor for detecting fluazinam. Moreover, the sensor could be utilized to analyze the fungicide in soil and apple samples and applied to construct visual paper-based sensing stripes. CDs have also been used to develop useful sensors for TNP determination, Wang et al. prepared a magnetic composite material of magnetic covalent organic frameworks, molecularly imprinted polymers and CDs, which resulted in excellent fluorescence properties and sensitivity for TNP with a LOD of 100 pM (Wang et al., 2020a). Gao et al. fabricated passivated CDs with carboxymethyl cellulose sodium (CMC) and 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) by a hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 720 min, which was further decorated with (3-chloro-2-hydroxypropyl)trimethylammonium chloride. This modified CDs was sensitive to TNP with a LOD of 7.04 × 10−7 molL−1 (Gao et al., 2018). Wang et al. designed and prepared a functional fluorescent probe with mesoporous SAB-15 modified with 3-glycidyloxypropyltrimethoxysilane and multi-hydroxyl CDs which was prepared with Tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane and citric acid, which exhibited very good selectivity and sensitivity to TNP (Wang et al., 2020b). Zhang et al. reported a fluorescence probe based on bright yellow emissive CDs from o-phenylenediamine and thiourea with a large Stokes shift (115 nm) by a simple one-pot solvothermal method (Zhang et al., 2020), which exhibited a LOD of 56 nM.

It was noteworthy that the efficiency of the methods based on FRET strongly relied on the distance between fluorophores and receptors (Lu et al., 2008; Chen et al., 2014), however the IFE system are not controlled by limited by this limit (Shang and Dong, 2009; Wang et al., 2017b). In IFE mechanism, the absorber's absorption overlaps with the fluorophore's emission band, which leads to the diminishment of the emission intensity resulting from non-radiative energy transformation. By contrast, the approaches based on IFE have many merits such as simplicity, considerable flexibility, high sensitivity and no covalent linkage (Wang et al., 2017b; Chang and Ho, 2015).

Based on the merits of CDs including strong fluorescence, good stability, and low toxicity, high sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescence sensors, and easy construction of IFE mechanism based sensors, the goal of present work is to design a new fluorescence sensor based on CDs for the determination of TNP. The sensors mentioned above for detecting TNP are based on modified CDs composite materials and the process was time-consumption. In present work, we prepared a fluorescent CDs from citric acid and polyacrylamide through a simple hydrothermal treatment, which had the emission peak of 415 nm along with the excitation peak of 320 nm. More importantly, the absorption spectrum of TNP overlapped both the excitation band and emission band of the synthesized fluorescent CDs (Ma et al., 2012). The synthesis of the fluorescent CDs and sensing principle of the sensor was illustrated in Fig. 1 . It is apparent that the fluorescence intensity of as-synthesized CDs can be dramatically quenched by TNP due to IFE. Therefore, a reliable and facile fluorescent sensor was developed for the sensitive determination of TNP.

Citric acid, acrylamide, ortho-nitrophenol (NP), ortho-nitrotoluene (NT), 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) and quinine sulfate were bought from Aladdin, China. 2,6-dinitrophenol (DNP) was bought from J&K, China. 2,6-Dinitrotoluene (DNT) and TNP were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, China. All other analytical-grade reagents were achieved from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company, China. Ultrapure water was utilized.

The polyacrylamide was first prepared. Acrylamide (5.0 g) was dissolved in 80 mL ultrapure water and heated for 15 min with stirring at room temperature. After then, 10 mL 2.5 mg mL−1 potassium persulfate solution was added into the solution, and reacted at 90 °C for 120 min with constant stirring. Then, the mixture was pre-concentrated with a rotary evaporator and dried. For the preparation of CDs, 0.521 g polyacrylamide and 0.966 g citric acid were mixed in 20 mL ultrapure water and then transferred to a PTFE reaction kettle, which was kept at 200 °C for 3 h and then cooled to ambient temperature. The obtained solution was dialyzed by a membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 1 kDa against ultrapure water for 3 h. The final gained solution containing CDs was yellowish-brown and dried. At last, the obtained CDs were used to prepare stocking solution of 50 μg mL−1 with ultrapure water for following experiments.

Fluorescence analysis was performed using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence lifetimes were examined using a FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, Edinburgh, UK). UV absorption was performed on a UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (SDPTOP, Shanghai, China). The morphologies and sizes were gained on a JEM-2100 TEM (JEOL, Akishima-shi, Japan) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was carried out on a Nicolet Magna-IR 750 spectrometer (Thermo, New York, USA). Relative fluorescence quantum yield was calculated according to reported method (Liu et al., 2015).

In a typical detection process, CDs (20 μL) solution and TNP solution at different levels were mixed in a centrifuge tube and then diluted to 1.0 mL with phosphate buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.0). All the fluorescence detections were conducted setting excitation wavelength at 320 nm with slit of 5 nm. The selectivity was investigated by adding certain amounts of DNP, NP, TNT, DNT and NT for TNP in a similar manner.

F our real water samples were collected from local region and before use they were treated with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. These samples were employed to explore the applicability and credibility of the constructed fluorescent sensor. These samples were analyzed at blank and spiked with different levels with this constructed sensor, respectively.

Fig. 2A shows the TEM image of the as-prepared CDs. It was obviously found that the as-prepared CDs were nearly spherical and wonderfully dispersed. The CDs were uniform and had a close size of 4.1 nm. Fig. 2B exhibits the FTIR spectrum of the obtained CDs. The three typical bands near 3418 cm−1, 3275 cm−1 and 1439 cm−1 were attributed to O-H/N-H, NH stretching vibration and OH bending vibration accordingly. The bands near 2945 cm−1 and 1716 cm−1 were ascribed to CH and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. While the bands centred at 1551 cm−1, 1223 cm−1 and 1169 cm−1 were due to N-H/C=C bending vibration, CN and CO stretching vibration accordingly (Miao et al., 2017). From FTIR results, it was apparent that there were many hydrophilic groups such as -OH, -NH and -COOH groups on the surface of the CDs, which not only enhanced the solubility of the prepared CDs in water and stability, but also contributed to form many more defects on CDs and which served as the excitation energy traps for emitting light effectively when photo-stimulated (Lai et al., 2013; Deng et al., 2014).

Fig. 3A exhibits the UV spectrum of the obtained CDs, which showed that CDs had two obvious absorption peaks. The strong absorption band near 230 nm was due to the π-π* transition of CC bond and the band near 325 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition which was closely related to the formation of the emissive traps and excited surface states resulting in strong fluorescence (Wang et al., 2014; Song et al., 2017). Fig. 3B shows the isogram or 3D diagram depending on excitation wavelength and emission wavelength, and the different colors represented the emission fluorescent intensity of the prepared CDs. It clearly indicated that the maximum emission of the prepared CDs occurred at 415 nm when the excitation wavelength was 320 nm. Fig. 3C clearly shows the excitation-independent multicolor emission feature. The fluorescence emission always appeared at 415 nm without blue-shifting or red-shifting regardless of the excitation wavelength changing in the range of 280–340 nm. Maybe it was ascribed to more same size of the prepared CDs, which was identical with the TEM view. The QY of the prepared CDs was 12.6% calculated based on the reported method (Liu et al., 2015), and which was markedly equal or better than some CDs that have been reported (Shu et al., 2017; Campos et al., 2016; Li et al., 2017).

Fig. 3D and E exhibits the change of fluorescence stability of the prepared CDs at different pH and ionic strength. It was obvious that the prepared CDs possessed strongly pH-sensitive property and inconspicuous change of the fluorescence intensity was found no matter how the ionic strength changed in the NaCl concentration range of 0–4.5 M, which affirmed that the prepared CDs owned high photostability and resistance to high ionic strength. In brief, the results revealed the CDs had a hopeful application prospect for sensing under physiological environment (Shi et al., 2015). Meanwhile, The CDs were rather stable for three months with negligible change of the fluorescence intensity (see Fig. 3F).

Fig. 4A shows that the presence of TNP can effectively quench the fluorescence emission intensity of the prepared CDs. Some important properties of the CDs and TNP were investigated to illuminate the sensing principle. It was clear that the absorption spectrum of TNP had great overlaps over the excitation spectrum as well as the emission spectrum of the prepared CDs (Fig. 4B), which opened up the possibility of IFE or FRET. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs with and without the presence of TNP were further explored (Fig. 4C). The average lifetimes of CDs, CDs+TNP were measured to be 8.65 and 8.83 ns, respectively. There was negligible change between their average lifetimes. As we know, in the IFE quenching system, the target analyte and the fluorescent CDs did not react chemically, and there was no significant change of the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescence CDs, which is different from FRET (Lin et al., 2015) and these data excluded the probability of FRET quenching mechanism in this sensing process. Hence, the fluorescence quenching mechanism should be supposed to be IFE quenching mechanism.

For gaining best detection sensitivity to TNP, several parameters involving the concentration of the prepared CDs, pH value of phosphate buffer solution and settling time were vital and further series of experiments were designed and carried out to obtain the optimal conditions. In the optimization procedure, the concentration of TNP was kept constant at 1.0 μg mL−1. The value of F0/F-1 (where F and F0 were fluorescent intensities of the prepared CDs with and without TNP, respectively.) was employed as the response for detecting TNP.

Generally, with given concentration of TNP, lower concentration of prepared CDs could enhance the response of TNP and gained higher sensitivity while the further higher concentration was beneficial to a wider detection range, but it also exhibited the weaker response of TNP in lower concentration range. Fig. 4D shows the influence of the concentration of the prepared CDs on detecting TNP, and the results indicated that a remarkable effect was present. The value of F0/F-1 significantly increased with the increase of the concentration of the CDs in the range of 0.5–1.0 μg mL−1 and later decreased with the continuous increase of the concentration of the CDs. So the optimal concentration of CDs was set at 1.0 μg mL−1.

Fig. 4E illustrates the influence of buffer pH on the F0/F-1 value. It was apparent that the value of F0/F-1 rose rapidly to a peak value with the change of buffer pH from pH 5 to pH 7.0 and later reduced with the continuous increase of buffer pH. Under strong acidic environment, some hydrogen ions in the system may react with TNP, which will result in weakening of the interaction between the prepared CDs and TNP. On the other hand, under alkali environment the interaction of TNP with OH– would lessen the reaction rate of TNP to CDs, and which could reduce the performance of the fluorescence recovery and result in the decrease of the value of F0/F-1. So, the optimal buffer pH was kept at pH 7.0 in subsequent experiments.

Fig. 4F shows the effect of the settling time. The value of F0/F-1 quickly increased to the maximum while the settling time went on in 2 min and almost remained unchanged with the continuous increase of time. For the sake of achieving stable signal, 3 min was selected as the optimum settling time.

Fig. 5A exhibits the fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs with the presence of TNP at different level over the range of 0.025–70 μg mL−1. It was found that excellent linearity was present between the value of F0/F-1 and the concentration of TNP in two ranges of 0.025–15 and 15–65 μg mL−1, and the correlation coefficients of the regression equations were 0.9998 and 0.9974, respectively (Fig. 6C, D). The sensitivity was very good to TNP with detection limit (LOD) of 5.37 ng mL−1 on the basis of LOD = 3σ/s, where σ was standard deviation of twenty blank measurements, and s represented the slope of the linear regression curve. The sensitivity of the constructed fluorescent sensor was compared with existed methods, and the results showed that present fluorescent probe was comparable with or superior to that of some reported methods (Table 1 ).

As the determination of TNP is concerned, it is an important characteristic of the fluorescent sensor that it should possess both high sensitivity and a good selectivity. The selectivity of the constructed fluorescent sensor was investigated by a series of experiments. Firstly, 0.25 μg mL−1 TNP and 2.5 μg mL−1 DNP, NP and 5.0 μg mL−1 TNT, DNT and NT were mixed with the CDs solution, respectively. Fig. 6 (blue bars) exhibited that no obvious changes of F0/F-1 value were obtained by above species except for TNP, which showed that the constructed fluorescent sensor owned a high selectivity and would be an competitive alternative for the determination of TNP. Secondly, the mixtures of 0.25 μg mL−1 TNP and the aforementioned nitroaromatics each were mixed with the CDs solution. The nitroaromatics resulted in no apparent effect on the strong quenching performance of TNP (Fig. 6, red bars). These results powerfully demonstrated that the developed fluorescent sensor possessed excellent selectivity for determination of TNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Precisions and stability for sensing TNP
ce:section.ce:para: Important parameters such as the precisions and stability were investigated. As intraday precision was concerned, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent sensor were recorded for six times a day with the concentration of TNP at three levels of 0.10, 0.25 and 0.50 μg mL−1. As inter day precision was concerned, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent sensor at aforementioned levels of TNP were acquired one time each day for six days totally. Based on the obtained data, the intraday precisions for 0.10, 0.25 and 0.50 μg mL−1 were calculated to be 4.0, 1.7 and 0.92% (RSD, n = 6), and the inter-day precisions for 0.10, 0.25 and 0.50 μg mL−1 were calculated to be 3.7, 1.4, and 1.4% (RSD, n = 6). What's more, the fluorescent signals of the constructed fluorescent sensor to the concentration of TNP at 0.50 μg mL−1 were collected at set intervals for 24 h. The results indicated that no significant changes were found within 24 h as exhibited in Fig. 7 . The experimental results demonstrated the fluorescent sensor had excellent stability, which confirmed the reproducibility of TNP determination with the fluorescent sensor.

It is of great value to validate the applicability of the developed fluorescent probe. Four real waters were collected and used to validate the probe. No TNP was found in the collected water samples with the constructed fluorescent sensor, and spiked samples were further analyzed. The results were shown in Table 2 . Obviously, good spiked recoveries of TNP were achieved ranged from 97.9 to 101.7% with RSDs in the range of 1.5–4.6%, which proved that the developed fluorescent sensor possessed high sensitivity and selectivity for TNP determination of, and could be used as a good alternative for the determination of TNP.

Present study described a novel fluorescent sensor with water-soluble CDs prepared from citrate acid and polyacrylamide using a facile and cheap hydrothermal process. The effective fluorescence sensor was successfully constructed for selective measurement of TNP based on IFE quenching mechanism. This sensor possessed good sensitivity with low LOD of 5.37 ng mL−1 under optimum conditions. The fluorescent sensor had been successfully applied for TNP detection in local water samples, and the spiked recoveries were satisfied. Therefore, the constructed sensor owned several advantages and would be a reliable and robust tool for sensing TNP in the samples.

Xin Wang: Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft.Yongli Liu: Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft.Qingxiang Zhou: Conceptualization, Supervision, Visualization, Investigation.Xueying Sheng: Resources.Yi Sun: Investigation.Boyao Zhou: Software.Jingyi Zhao: Writing - review & editing.Jinghan Guo: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21976211).
